title: Waldorf salad
subtitle: Classic American salad of apple, celery, walnut and mayonnaise.
excerpt: Crisp eating apple, celery, walnuts and raisins bound in a lightly dressed mayonnaise and served on lettuce. The original Waldorf salad from the 1890s, unchanged. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Waldorf salad. Created in 1896 by Oscar Tschirky, the maître d'hôtel of the Waldorf-Astoria Hotel in New York. The original recipe contained only apple, celery and mayonnaise. Walnuts were added by subsequent versions. The recipe appeared in The Epicurean by Charles Ranhofer (1894, public domain) and was published widely throughout the early 20th century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 hours ahead and refrigerate. Add walnuts just before serving to keep them crisp.

content.text: Waldorf salad is about texture as much as flavour: the crunch of celery and walnut against the soft apple, bound in a light mayonnaise. Use a good-flavoured eating apple — Cox or Braeburn rather than a supermarket Gala. Toss the cut apple in lemon juice immediately after cutting to prevent browning.

items.prepNote: juice only, to prevent browning and dress

content.text: Dice {{apple-eating}} of eating apple and toss immediately with the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Add {{celery}} of sliced celery stalks, {{raisins}} of raisins and {{walnuts}} of walnuts. Mix {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise with {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Fold the dressing through the salad until everything is coated. Serve on a bed of lettuce leaves if using.

items.cause: Not tossed in lemon juice immediately after cutting.
items.fix: Dress the apple in lemon juice the moment it is cut. Work quickly; once the flesh is exposed to air, browning begins.

items.cause: Full mayonnaise without the yoghurt to lighten.
items.fix: The yoghurt thins the dressing and adds a faint acidity that prevents it from feeling cloying.

items.cause: Salad made too far ahead with walnuts already in.
items.fix: Add the walnuts no more than 30 minutes before serving.

content.text: The Waldorf salad is one of the few hotel dishes that entered the domestic repertoire unchanged. The combination has lasted 130 years because it works: sweet, crisp, nutty and lightly dressed. It belongs on a buffet table, next to cold chicken or ham, or as a lunch on its own. The yoghurt twist is modern but appropriate.
